topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the screen, the glow of the monitor reflecting in his tired eyes. Another line of code, another iteration of the algorithm that felt utterly meaningless. He leaned back in his worn office chair, the springs groaning in protest beneath his weight. The air in the room was thick, stagnant, mirroring the fog settling over his mind. He rubbed his temples, the pressure behind his eyes a constant, dull ache. Then, a flicker of recognition. He scrolled back through the shared drive, and there it was: the exact same complex data structure, the same peculiar, frustratingly elegant solution. A cold, heavy feeling washed over him, settling deep in his chest. He felt a sudden, profound exhaustion, as if the very effort of creation had drained the last reserves of his being. He closed his laptop with a soft, definitive click, the sound echoing loudly in the silence.

[story 2]

Maya traced the lines of the blueprint with a fingertip, the paper cool beneath her touch. The silence of the studio was oppressive, amplifying the frantic rhythm of her own thoughts. She hadn't slept properly in days, the edges of reality blurring into a monotonous grey. Every sketch she made felt hollow, a desperate attempt to fill a void that seemed cavernous and bottomless. When she cross-referenced the final schematic with the file she’d been obsessively refining, a jolt, sharp and unwelcome, shot through her. The design was identical. A wave of nausea rolled over her, making her grip the edge of the desk until her knuckles turned white. She let out a slow, shaky breath, the effort of maintaining composure suddenly too much. She just wanted the noise to stop.

[story 3]

The hum of the server room was the only constant in Alex’s world, a low, irritating drone that seemed to vibrate directly against his skull. He was staring at the progress bar, stuck at 99.9%, a perfect, infuriating stalemate. His shoulders slumped forward, a physical manifestation of the weight he carried. He’d been pouring everything into this project—years of meticulous planning, sleepless nights fueled by lukewarm coffee, all leading to this dead end. Then, a notification popped up, linking to a repository he’d forgotten existed. There, nested in a folder labeled ‘Final Submission,’ was the exact same, utterly familiar architecture. A strange hollowness expanded in his stomach. He pushed himself up from his desk, the movement sluggish, heavy. He didn't move to delete it; he just stood there, feeling utterly adrift, as if the ground beneath him had dissolved.
